content.problemStatement:
Given an integer array numsnumsnums where **every number appears exactly twice except for two distinct numbers**, your task is to **identify the two numbers that occur only once**.
All other numbers appear in pairs.
Return the two unique numbers **in ascending order**.

Function DeclarationFunction NamefindSingleNumbersfindSingleNumbersfindSingleNumbers – This function determines the two integers that appear exactly once in an array where all other integers appear twice.

Parameters
numsnumsnums: A list of integers representing the array elements.
Return Value
Returns a list of **two integers** that appear only once, sorted in **ascending order**.
`The input and output formats below are only if you want to test using custom inputs.`

Constraints
2≤nums.length≤1052 \leq nums.length \leq 10^52≤nums.length≤105
−3×105≤nums[i]≤3×105-3 \times 10^5 \leq nums[i] \leq 3 \times 10^5−3×105≤nums[i]≤3×105
Exactly two integers appear once; all others appear twice.

Input Format


The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.




Each test case consists of:



A single line containing comma-separated integers representing the array numsnumsnums.




Output Format

For each test case, print the two unique integers in ascending order inside brackets.
Example:
[a,b][a, b][a,b]
constraints: Exactly two integers appear once; all others appear twice
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single line with a comma-separated list of integers representing the array elements.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the two unique numbers in ascending order, enclosed in square brackets with a comma and a space separating them.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In each test case, all numbers appear twice except for two distinct numbers. The algorithm isolates those two numbers and prints them sorted.
First case: both numbers are unique. Second case: 50 appears twice, leaving 100 and -100 as the unique values.
0 and 5 appear twice; -1 and 7 appear once, printed in ascending order.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using bitwise XOR operations. First, we XOR all the numbers in the array to find the XOR of the two unique numbers. Then, we find the rightmost set bit in the XOR result, which will be set in one of the unique numbers and not in the other. We use this bit to divide the numbers into two groups and XOR each group separately to find the two unique numbers.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T * N) total, where N is the length of the input array and T is the number of test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1), excluding the space required for the input and output.
